transcript: Speaker A: Do you speak English? Speaker B: Yes. Speaker A: Prove it. Speaker B: Um, I'm here to visit my sister in Miami. I'm staying for two weeks. I have the return ticket, I'm not working, just visiting. Speaker A: Alright, how? Speaker B: I used Loora. Ten minutes a day, for real conversations like this one. Let me show you how it works. Speaker C: Hello, where are you flying? Speaker B: I fly London. Speaker C: Do you mean "I am flying to London"? Speaker B: Yes, sorry. I am flying to London. Speaker C: Any luggage to check in? Speaker B: Mm, luggage? Speaker C: Yes, luggage. It might be bags, suitcases, and other containers people use to carry their belongings when traveling. Speaker B: Oh, I have one luggage. Sorry, I'm not so good English. Speaker C: Do you mean "My English isn't that good"? No worries. How many times have you spoken in English over the past month? Speaker B: Mm, actually I didn't. Speaker C: So how do you expect to speak fluently? Speaker B: I not have anyone to practice with. Speaker C: Just download Loora. We'll speak together daily and you'll be fluent in no time. Speaker B: That's amazing. I'm adding this to my daily routine today.

summary: A woman demonstrates her fluent English at passport control, attributing her success to the Loora app. She showcases a split-screen demo where an AI avatar corrects her grammar and vocabulary in real-time. The ad promotes downloading Loora for daily practice to achieve fluency.
